options: Like a pin, but the more valuable piece is in front and must move first
A piece moving in a zigzag pattern
Two pieces attacking the same square
A pawn capturing two pieces at once
questions.explanation: A skewer is the reverse of a pin: a valuable piece (usually the king) is in front, and a less valuable piece is directly behind. When the valuable piece moves out of the way, the behind-piece is exposed.

questions.prompt: The white bishop on c1 can skewer the black king on e3 (in front) and the black rook on g5 (behind). Tap the square the bishop should move to.
questions.explanation: Bd2! The bishop steps to d2, on the c1-h6 diagonal. It attacks the king on e3; when the king moves out of the way, the rook on g5 is exposed and falls.

questions.prompt: What's the key difference between a pin and a skewer?
options: A pin has the more valuable piece in BACK (it can't move); a skewer has it in FRONT (it must move)
A pin only works with rooks; a skewer only works with bishops
A pin is in the opening; a skewer is in the endgame
questions.explanation: Pin: valuable piece is in BACK, frozen (can't expose the king). Skewer: valuable piece is in FRONT, forced to move and reveal the weaker piece behind.
